result.markdown:
# Joining data

We cover the two main ways to combine separate tables into one: horizontal joining — bringing in new columns by matching rows on a shared key — and vertical stacking for combining tables that share the same columns.

{{< video https://vimeo.com/1195921351/acdd0ad4f7?fl=ml&fe=ec >}}

```python
import pandas as pd

healthexp = pd.read_csv("data/healthexp.csv")
healthexp.shape
healthexp.head()
healthexp["Country"].unique()
healthexp["Year"].nunique()

# Filter to a single year so each country appears only once
healthexp_2020 = healthexp[healthexp["Year"] == 2020].copy()
healthexp_2020

# ── Loading a CSV from a URL ─────────────────────────────────

# Repo: https://github.com/owid/owid-datasets/blob/master/datasets/Countries%20Continents/Countries%20Continents.csv
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/owid/owid-datasets/refs/heads/master/datasets/Countries%20Continents/Countries%20Continents.csv"

continents = pd.read_csv(url)
continents.head()
continents["Entity"].nunique()

# ── Preparing the data for merging ───────────────────────────

continents = continents.drop(columns=["Year"])
continents = continents.rename(columns={"Entity": "Country"})
continents.head()

# ── Inner join ───────────────────────────────────────────────

merged_inner = healthexp_2020.merge(continents, on="Country", how="inner")
merged_inner

# ── Left join ────────────────────────────────────────────────

merged_left = healthexp_2020.merge(continents, on="Country", how="left")
merged_left

# ── Combining DataFrames vertically with concat() ────────────

titanic = pd.read_csv("data/titanic.csv")

# Split into two subsets
men   = titanic[titanic["sex"] == "male"].copy()
women = titanic[titanic["sex"] == "female"].copy()

print("Men:  ", len(men))
print("Women:", len(women))

# Stack them back into one DataFrame
combined = pd.concat([men, women])
print("Combined:", len(combined))
combined.head()

# Reset the index
combined = pd.concat([men, women]).reset_index(drop=True)
combined.head()

```

## Exercises

### Exercise 3.8.1

The two DataFrames below contain exam scores for a group of students. Not all students took both exams.

```python
import pandas as pd

exam1 = pd.DataFrame({
    "student_id": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
    "name":       ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eva"],
    "score_q1":   [85, 92, 78, 90, 88]
})

exam2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "student_id": [1, 2, 3, 6, 7],
    "name":       ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Frank", "Grace"],
    "score_q2":   [90, 88, 95, 72, 85]
})
```

Merge the two DataFrames using all four join types — `inner`, `left`, `right`, and `outer` — and answer the following questions for each:

1. How many rows does the result have?
2. Which students appear in the result, and which are excluded?
3. Where do `NaN` values appear, and why?

Summarize in your own words when you would choose each join type.

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

::: {#3204e267 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd

exam1 = pd.DataFrame({
    "student_id": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
    "name":       ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eva"],
    "score_q1":   [85, 92, 78, 90, 88]
})

exam2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "student_id": [1, 2, 3, 6, 7],
    "name":       ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "Frank", "Grace"],
    "score_q2":   [90, 88, 95, 72, 85]
})

# Inner join
print("Inner:")
print(exam1.merge(exam2, on="student_id", how="inner"))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Inner:
   student_id name_x  score_q1 name_y  score_q2
0           1  Alice        85  Alice        90
1           2    Bob        92    Bob        88
2           3  Carol        78  Carol        95
```
:::
:::


::: {#cd062f0a .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Left join
print("Left:")
print(exam1.merge(exam2, on="student_id", how="left"))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Left:
   student_id name_x  score_q1 name_y  score_q2
0           1  Alice        85  Alice      90.0
1           2    Bob        92    Bob      88.0
2           3  Carol        78  Carol      95.0
3           4  David        90    NaN       NaN
4           5    Eva        88    NaN       NaN
```
:::
:::


::: {#88f253bc .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Right join
print("Right:")
print(exam1.merge(exam2, on="student_id", how="right"))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Right:
   student_id name_x  score_q1 name_y  score_q2
0           1  Alice      85.0  Alice        90
1           2    Bob      92.0    Bob        88
2           3  Carol      78.0  Carol        95
3           6    NaN       NaN  Frank        72
4           7    NaN       NaN  Grace        85
```
:::
:::


::: {#4ecb7460 .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# Outer join
print("Outer:")
print(exam1.merge(exam2, on="student_id", how="outer"))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Outer:
   student_id name_x  score_q1 name_y  score_q2
0           1  Alice      85.0  Alice      90.0
1           2    Bob      92.0    Bob      88.0
2           3  Carol      78.0  Carol      95.0
3           4  David      90.0    NaN       NaN
4           5    Eva      88.0    NaN       NaN
5           6    NaN       NaN  Frank      72.0
6           7    NaN       NaN  Grace      85.0
```
:::
:::


Notice that merging on `student_id` produces duplicate `name` columns — `name_x` from the left table and `name_y` from the right. pandas adds suffixes automatically when both tables have a column with the same name that is not used as the merge key. You can avoid this by dropping the `name` column from one table before merging, or by merging on both `student_id` and `name` by passing a list of key columns to `on`.

- **Inner**: 3 rows — only students 1, 2, 3 who appear in both tables. Students 4, 5, 6, 7 are silently dropped. Use when you only want observations present in both tables.
- **Left**: 5 rows — all students from `exam1`. Students 6 and 7 are excluded; students 4 and 5 have `NaN` for `score_q2`. Use when your left table is the main dataset and you want to keep all of it.
- **Right**: 5 rows — all students from `exam2`. Students 4 and 5 are excluded; students 6 and 7 have `NaN` for `score_q1`. Use when the right table is the one you want to preserve completely.
- **Outer**: 7 rows — all students from both tables. `NaN` appears wherever a student is missing from one of the tables. Use when you want to keep everything and investigate what is missing on either side.

</details>

---

### Exercise 3.8.2

In the previous exercise, merging on `student_id` alone produced duplicate `name` columns because both tables contained a `name` column that was not used as the key. One clean solution is to merge on multiple keys.

A colleague provides a second version of the exam data where the student names
are stored differently:

```python
import pandas as pd

exam1 = pd.DataFrame({
    "student_id": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
    "first_name": ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eva"],
    "last_name":  ["Smith", "Jones", "White", "Brown", "Davis"],
    "score_q1":   [85, 92, 78, 90, 88]
})

exam2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "student_id": [1, 2, 3, 6, 7],
    "name":       ["alice smith", "bob jones", "carol white",
                   "frank miller", "grace wilson"],
    "score_q2":   [90, 88, 95, 72, 85]
})
```

The names in `exam2` are in lowercase and stored as a single `name` column, while `exam1` splits them across `first_name` and `last_name`.

1. Add a `name` column to `exam1` by combining `first_name` and `last_name` into a single lowercase string in the format `"alice smith"`. Use the `.str` accessor.
2. Merge the two DataFrames on both `student_id` and `name` using a left join. How does the result differ from merging on `student_id` alone?
3. Drop the `first_name` and `last_name` columns from the result and print the final DataFrame.

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

::: {#b1f1575a .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd

exam1 = pd.DataFrame({
    "student_id": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
    "first_name": ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eva"],
    "last_name":  ["Smith", "Jones", "White", "Brown", "Davis"],
    "score_q1":   [85, 92, 78, 90, 88]
})

exam2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "student_id": [1, 2, 3, 6, 7],
    "name":       ["alice smith", "bob jones", "carol white",
                   "frank miller", "grace wilson"],
    "score_q2":   [90, 88, 95, 72, 85]
})

# 1. Combine first and last name into a single lowercase column
exam1["name"] = (exam1["first_name"] + " " + exam1["last_name"]).str.lower()
print(exam1)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
   student_id first_name last_name  score_q1         name
0           1      Alice     Smith        85  alice smith
1           2        Bob     Jones        92    bob jones
2           3      Carol     White        78  carol white
3           4      David     Brown        90  david brown
4           5        Eva     Davis        88    eva davis
```
:::
:::


::: {#2ab8b18e .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 2. Merge on both student_id and name
merged = exam1.merge(exam2, on=["student_id", "name"], how="left")
print(merged)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
   student_id first_name last_name  score_q1         name  score_q2
0           1      Alice     Smith        85  alice smith      90.0
1           2        Bob     Jones        92    bob jones      88.0
2           3      Carol     White        78  carol white      95.0
3           4      David     Brown        90  david brown       NaN
4           5        Eva     Davis        88    eva davis       NaN
```
:::
:::


::: {#f56524db .cell execution_count=7}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 3. Drop first_name and last_name
merged = merged.drop(columns=["first_name", "last_name"])
print(merged)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
   student_id  score_q1         name  score_q2
0           1        85  alice smith      90.0
1           2        92    bob jones      88.0
2           3        78  carol white      95.0
3           4        90  david brown       NaN
4           5        88    eva davis       NaN
```
:::
:::


**Task 2:** Merging on both keys avoids the duplicate name column problem from exercise 3.8.1. When two tables share a column that you want to use as a key rather than a data column, include it in the `on=` list. This is also safer: merging on multiple keys reduces the risk of false matches where two different people happen to share the same student ID or the same name.

</details>

---

### Exercise 3.8.3

The code below merges two DataFrames that track product sales. Run it and inspect the result carefully.

```python
import pandas as pd

products = pd.DataFrame({
    "product_id":   [1, 2, 3],
    "product_name": ["Coffee", "Tea", "Juice"]
})

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "product_id": [1, 1, 2, 2, 3],
    "month":      ["Jan", "Feb", "Jan", "Feb", "Jan"],
    "units_sold": [120, 135, 80, 90, 60]
})

merged = products.merge(sales, on="product_id", how="left")
print("Products rows:", len(products))
print("Merged rows:  ", len(merged))
print(merged)
```

1. How many rows does `merged` have compared to `products`? Why?
2. Is this the result you would expect from a left join? What has happened?
3. How would you check for duplicate keys in `sales` before performing the merge? Write the code to detect them.
4. In this case, the extra rows are not a mistake — `sales` intentionally has multiple rows per product. How would you restructure the merge to get one row per product that includes both the product name and total units sold across all months?

<details>
<summary>Hint</summary>

For question 4, consider aggregating `sales` before merging rather than merging first and aggregating after.

</details>

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

::: {#c10ed2af .cell execution_count=8}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd

products = pd.DataFrame({
    "product_id":   [1, 2, 3],
    "product_name": ["Coffee", "Tea", "Juice"]
})

sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "product_id": [1, 1, 2, 2, 3],
    "month":      ["Jan", "Feb", "Jan", "Feb", "Jan"],
    "units_sold": [120, 135, 80, 90, 60]
})

merged = products.merge(sales, on="product_id", how="left")
print("Products rows:", len(products))
print("Merged rows:  ", len(merged))
print(merged)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Products rows: 3
Merged rows:   5
   product_id product_name month  units_sold
0           1       Coffee   Jan         120
1           1       Coffee   Feb         135
2           2          Tea   Jan          80
3           2          Tea   Feb          90
4           3        Juice   Jan          60
```
:::
:::


::: {#597066f3 .cell execution_count=9}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 3. Check for duplicate keys in sales
print(sales["product_id"].value_counts())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
product_id
1    2
2    2
3    1
Name: count, dtype: int64
```
:::
:::


::: {#b760353c .cell execution_count=10}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 4. Aggregate sales first, then merge
sales_total = sales.groupby("product_id")["units_sold"].sum().reset_index()
merged_clean = products.merge(sales_total, on="product_id", how="left")
print(merged_clean)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
   product_id product_name  units_sold
0           1       Coffee         255
1           2          Tea         170
2           3        Juice          60
```
:::
:::


1. `merged` has 5 rows — more than the 3 rows in `products`. Each product in `products` has been duplicated once for each matching row in `sales`.

2. This is technically correct behaviour for a left join — every matching row in the right table generates a new row in the result. But it is often not what you want, and if you were not expecting it, the inflated row count is a sign that something needs attention.

3. `.value_counts()` on the key column quickly shows whether any values appear more than once. In real data with thousands of rows, you can also check `sales["product_id"].duplicated().sum()` for a quick count of duplicate keys.

4. Aggregating before merging is cleaner: first collapse `sales` to one row per product, then merge. This is a general principle — if the right table has multiple rows per key and you want a one-to-one merge, aggregate first.

</details>

---

### Exercise 3.8.4

Split the `flights` dataset by month using a for loop, then reassemble it using `pd.concat()`.

```python
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")
print(flights["month"].unique())
```

1. Use a for loop to split `flights` into 12 subsets — one for each calendar month (e.g., `Jan`) — and store them in a list called `month_dfs`. Print the shape of each DataFrame in the list.
2. Use `pd.concat()` to reassemble the list into a single DataFrame called `flights_combined`. Reset the index with `drop=True`.
3. Verify the reassembled DataFrame matches the original by checking that the shapes are identical and that the total passenger count is the same.

<details>
<summary>Hint</summary>

To split by month using a for loop, define a list of calendar months and loop over them. For each month, filter the DataFrame to rows where `month` is equal to that month and append the result the list. Recall that you can use the `append` function to add items to a list inside the loop.

</details>

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

::: {#6a07859c .cell execution_count=11}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd

flights = pd.read_csv("data/flights.csv")

# 1. Split by month using a for loop
month_dfs = []

for month in flights["month"].unique():
    subset = flights[flights["month"] == month].copy()
    month_dfs.append(subset)
    print(subset.shape)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
(12, 3)
```
:::
:::


::: {#b018daac .cell execution_count=12}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 2. Reassemble with pd.concat()
flights_combined = pd.concat(month_dfs).reset_index(drop=True)
print(flights_combined.shape)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
(144, 3)
```
:::
:::


::: {#d0dfa040 .cell execution_count=13}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 3. Verify against original
print(flights.shape == flights_combined.shape)
print(flights["passengers"].sum() == flights_combined["passengers"].sum())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
True
True
```
:::
:::


The key pattern here is storing DataFrames in a list inside a loop and then passing the list to `pd.concat()`. This is a common pattern in real data work — for example when reading multiple CSV files from a folder, each containing data for a different time period, and assembling them into a single DataFrame. Always reset the index after `pd.concat()` to avoid duplicate index labels from
the original DataFrames.

</details>

---

### Exercise 3.8.5

In this exercise you will merge two real datasets hosted on [GitHub](https://github.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/tree/master/03-data-wrangling/data): one containing core passenger information from `titanic` and one containing passenger names and ticket details. Both datasets originate from the same source and share `passenger_id` as the primary key, but they also share two other columns — `Pclass` and `Sex` — with different casing.

```python
import pandas as pd

url_core  = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_core.csv"
url_names = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_names.csv"

core  = pd.read_csv(url_core)
names = pd.read_csv(url_names)

print(core.shape)
print(core.head())
print(names.shape)
print(names.head())
```

1. Inspect both DataFrames. Which columns appear in both? How does the casing differ, and why does this matter for merging?
2. Rename `PassengerId`, `Pclass`, and `Sex` in `names` to `passenger_id`, `pclass`, and `sex` so the column names match `core`.
3. Merge the two DataFrames using a left join on `passenger_id`. Then drop the duplicate `pclass` and `sex` columns that result from the merge. Check the row count after merging to verify no rows were lost.
4. Using the merged DataFrame, compute the survival rate by passenger class for male and female passengers separately. Which group had the highest survival rate?

<details>
<summary>Hint</summary>

After merging on `passenger_id` alone, pandas will suffix the duplicate `pclass` and `sex` columns as `pclass_x`, `pclass_y`, `sex_x`, and `sex_y`. You can drop the `_y` versions and rename the `_x` versions back, or alternatively merge on all three shared keys — `passenger_id`, `pclass`, and `sex` — to avoid the duplicates entirely.

</details>

<details>
<summary>Solution</summary>

::: {#8409f6c2 .cell execution_count=14}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import pandas as pd

url_core  = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_core.csv"
url_names = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/refs/heads/master/03-data-wrangling/data/titanic_names.csv"

core  = pd.read_csv(url_core)
names = pd.read_csv(url_names)

# 2. Rename columns in names to match core
names = names.rename(columns={
    "PassengerId": "passenger_id",
    "Pclass":      "pclass",
    "Sex":         "sex"
})
```
:::


::: {#fee047fe .cell execution_count=15}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 3. Merge on all three shared keys to avoid duplicate columns
merged = core.merge(names, on=["passenger_id", "pclass", "sex"], how="left")
print("Core rows:  ", len(core))
print("Merged rows:", len(merged))
print(merged.head())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Core rows:   891
Merged rows: 891
   passenger_id  survived  pclass     sex   age     fare embarked  \
0             1         0       3    male  22.0   7.2500        S   
1             2         1       1  female  38.0  71.2833        C   
2             3         1       3  female  26.0   7.9250        S   
3             4         1       1  female  35.0  53.1000        S   
4             5         0       3    male  35.0   8.0500        S   

                                                Name            Ticket Cabin  
0                            Braund, Mr. Owen Harris         A/5 21171   NaN  
1  Cumings, Mrs. John Bradley (Florence Briggs Th...          PC 17599   C85  
2                             Heikkinen, Miss. Laina  STON/O2. 3101282   NaN  
3       Futrelle, Mrs. Jacques Heath (Lily May Peel)            113803  C123  
4                           Allen, Mr. William Henry            373450   NaN  
```
:::
:::


::: {#d93ec095 .cell execution_count=16}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 4. Survival rate by class and sex
summary = merged.groupby(["pclass", "sex"])["survived"].mean().round(3)
print(summary)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
pclass  sex   
1       female    0.968
        male      0.369
2       female    0.921
        male      0.157
3       female    0.500
        male      0.135
Name: survived, dtype: float64
```
:::
:::


**Task 1:** Both DataFrames contain `passenger_id`/`PassengerId`, `pclass`/`Pclass`, and `sex`/`Sex`. The casing difference matters because pandas treats column names as case-sensitive — `pclass` and `Pclass` are different column names and will not be recognised as the same key unless you standardise them first.

**Task 3:** Merging on all three shared keys is the cleanest approach — it avoids duplicate columns entirely and provides an additional check that the rows match correctly across all three variables. The row count should be 891 in both `core` and `merged`, confirming no rows were lost.

**Task 4:** First class female passengers had by far the highest survival rate at around 97%. In general, female passengers survived at much higher rates than male passengers across all classes, and higher passenger classes survived at higher rates than lower classes — with the exception that second class female passengers also survived at very high rates.

</details>
